text: The video shows a robotic paraesophageal hernia repair with Toupee fundoplication. A large diaphragmatic hernia defect is visible with the stomach herniated into the chest. The surgeon makes an incision in the peritoneum at the one o'clock position, extending it to the left. A bloodless plane is identified to sweep the hernia sac away from the endothoracic fascia. The beating heart is visible above, covered by the pericardium. The gastrohepatic ligament is incised, preserving the left gastric artery and an accessory left hepatic artery. The hernia sac is grasped and retracted downward, with adhesions divided between it and the left pleura.

The video shows a robotic paraesophageal hernia repair with toupee fundoplication. A large diaphragmatic hernia defect is visible, with the stomach herniated into the chest. The surgeon makes an incision in the peritoneum over the hiatal defect at the one o'clock position, extending it to the left side. The hernia sac is swept down from the endothoracic fascia, revealing the beating heart covered by the pericardium. The gastrohepatic ligament is incised and opened widely, preserving the left gastric artery and an accessory left hepatic artery. The hernia sac is grasped and retracted downward, with adhesions divided between it and the left pleura, the space containing the lung.

The surgeon completes the initial dissection of the anterior half of the hernia and proceeds down the greater curvature of the stomach. Using a vessel sealer, the surgeon enters the lesser sac behind the stomach and carries the dissection upward towards the esophagus, dividing short gastric and posterior gastric vessels. This mobilizes the upper part of the stomach, including the fundus, for later fundoplication, while ensuring hemostasis with the advanced energy device.

The surgeon completes the initial dissection of the anterior half of the hernia and proceeds about a third to halfway down the greater curvature of the stomach. Using a vessel sealer, the surgeon enters the lesser sac behind the stomach and carries the dissection upward towards the esophagus. The vessel sealer is used to divide the short gastric and posterior gastric vessels, fully mobilizing the upper part of the stomach, including the fundus. The surgeon emphasizes the importance of using a reliable energy source to manage potential bleeding. The lower esophagus and upper stomach are encircled with a Penrose drain for control.

The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to perform precise blunt dissection around the lower esophagus and upper stomach. A Penrose drain is passed to encircle and control the hiatal contents, aiding in effective retraction. The hernia sac is dissected posteriorly off the cruse, with extensive mediastinal mobilization to free the esophagus from surrounding tissue, facilitating the stomach's repositioning below the diaphragm.

The surgeon uses articulated robotic instruments to dissect the hernia sac posteriorly, separating it from the cruse. The dissection is carried high into the mediastinum to extensively mobilize the esophagus, freeing it from surrounding tissue. This maneuver aids in repositioning the stomach below the diaphragm, reducing the risk of hernia recurrence.

The surgeon dissects the hernia sac from the esophagus and gastroesophageal junction, carefully removing it from the body. The final portion of the hernia sac is teased apart from the right cruce, avoiding entry into the right pleural space. The external hiatal dissection and mediastinal mobilization are completed, revealing that the crura come together well without the need for mesh. The surgeon checks the intraabdominal length of the esophagus, ensuring the gastroesophageal junction is positioned below the diaphragm.

The surgeon dissects the hernia sac from the esophagus and gastroesophageal junction, carefully removing it from the body. The final portion of the hernia sac is teased apart from the right cruce, avoiding entry into the right pleural space. The external hiatal dissection and mediastinal mobilization are completed, revealing that the crura come together well, negating the need for mesh. Xeroethabones are introduced into the abdomen for the hiatal hernia repair.

The surgeon measures the intraabdominal length of the esophagus, ensuring the gastroesophageal junction is positioned well below the diaphragm. The hiatal hernia repair is performed using several interrupted sutures of zero ethebond, with the muscular pillars of the diaphragm coming together without excessive tension.

The surgeon begins the hiatal hernia repair using several interrupted sutures of zero ethebond to bring the muscular pillars of the diaphragm together without excessive tension. The robotic assistance enhances the fluidity of the movements during the suturing process.

The surgeon performs a hiatal hernia repair using robotic assistance, making fluid maneuvers to address tension in the muscle. A semi-relaxing incision is made along the left side using a vessel sealer, facilitating the muscle's midline approximation.

The surgeon observes tension in the hiatal hernia repair and makes a semi-relaxing incision along the left side using a vessel sealer. This incision allows the muscle to come together at the midline more easily. The surgeon assesses the esophagus filling the hiatal defect and decides to place two sutures anteriorly to close the hiatus to a normal size, avoiding excessive angulation that could impede swallowing.

The surgeon assesses the esophagus filling the hiatal defect and places two sutures anteriorly to close the hiatus to a normal size, ensuring minimal angulation on the esophagus to prevent swallowing difficulties.

The surgeon performs a partial posterior fundoplication by passing the fundus behind the esophagus from left to right, using a shoeshine maneuver to ensure symmetry and prevent twisting of the gastroesophageal junction. The wrap is anchored with a gastropexy, suturing the backside of the wrap to the crural closure to secure the fundoplication and prevent recurrent hiatal hernia.

The surgeon completes the hiatal hernia repair and begins the partial posterior fundoplication by passing the fundus behind the esophagus from left to right. The surgeon performs a shoeshine maneuver to ensure the wrap is symmetric and the gastroesophageal junction is not twisted, aiming for an optimal anti-reflux outcome.

The surgeon performs a gastropexy by suturing the backside of the fundoplication wrap to the crural closure, anchoring it within the abdomen to prevent a recurrent hiatal hernia. The partial posterior fundoplication is completed, taking on a hot dog-like appearance. Intraoperative endoscopy is used to check the appearance, revealing an omega loop shape internally. The surgeon checks for hemostasis and confirms the position of the gastroesophageal junction and fundoplication level with the endoscope light visible.

The surgeon completes the 270-degree partial posterior fundoplication, giving it a hot dog-like appearance. Intraoperative endoscopy is used to verify the omega loop appearance internally, with the light from the scope illuminating the gastroesophageal junction and the level of the fundoplication. The surgeon checks for hemostasis to ensure the surgical site is stable.
